pattern_recognition: Matrix Manipulation; this problem involves altering a matrix based on specific conditions, which is a common pattern in problems involving grids or 2D arrays.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Iterate through the matrix, and for each zero found, mark the entire row and column for zeroing later.
approaches.algorithm: Use a temporary matrix to store the positions of zeros, then iterate through the original matrix to set the marked rows and columns to zero.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use the first row and first column of the matrix itself to mark which rows and columns need to be zeroed, thus saving space.
approaches.algorithm: First, determine if the first row and first column need to be zeroed. Then, use the first row and column to mark other rows and columns that should be zeroed. Finally, zero out the marked rows and columns.

dry_run: Consider the input matrix: [[1, 1, 1], [1, 0, 1], [1, 1, 1]]. Start by identifying the zeros. The zero is located at (1, 1). Mark the first row and column accordingly. After marking, the matrix looks like: [[1, 0, 1], [0, 0, 0], [1, 0, 1]]. Finally, zero out the marked rows and columns, resulting in: [[1, 0, 1], [0, 0, 0], [1, 0, 1]].
edge_cases: An empty matrix should return an empty matrix. A single element matrix with zero should return [[0]], while a single element matrix without zero should return the same element. Duplicates should be handled correctly, ensuring all relevant rows and columns are zeroed. Large matrices should be managed without performance issues, and overflow should be considered if large integers are involved.
common_pitfalls: 1) Forgetting to handle the first row and column separately can lead to incorrect results. 2) Not considering the case where the entire matrix needs to be zeroed can cause errors. 3) Using additional space unnecessarily when an in-place solution is required can lead to inefficiency.
